rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical oxidative synthesis of tetralones from styrenes (olefins) and water using a platinum nanoparticle anode, proceeding via radical cation intermediate and intramolecular Friedel-Crafts annulation
electrochemical_role: Anodic single-electron oxidation of styrene to a styryl radical cation; second anodic oxidation of a benzylic radical to a benzylic carbocation; cathodic reduction of protons to H2

key_intermediates.name: Wheland-type cyclohexadienyl radical cation from Friedel-Crafts annulation (C)

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes styrene by SET to a radical cation, which couples with a second styrene to give a distal 1,4-radical cation; intramolecular Friedel-Crafts annulation and -H+ yields a benzylic radical, which is further oxidized at the anode to a carbocation that is trapped by water; subsequent oxidation/deprotonation gives the tetralone, while protons are reduced to H2 at the cathode.
rate_determining_step: Initial anodic single-electron oxidation of the styrene to its radical cation (consistent with measured oxidation potential ~1.96 V vs SHE and DFT 1.922 V)

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction significantly inhibited; TEMPO-adduct 3v detected by HRMS, supporting a radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE)
probe_reagents.observation: Radical addition product 3x detected by HRMS, indicating a styryl/benzylic radical species

probe_reagents.name: tetralin-1-ol intermediate 3a'
probe_reagents.observation: Isolated in 17% yield at half reaction time; resubjection under standard conditions delivered tetralone 3a in 95% yield, confirming it as on-pathway

probe_reagents.name: H2(18)O
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into product 3a'' (HRMS), proving the carbonyl oxygen comes from water, not O2

notes: CV shows two oxidative overpotentials (+1.74 V and +2.17 V vs Ag/AgCl), consistent with sequential SET steps (styrene -> radical cation; benzylic radical D -> carbocation E). DFT computes substrate oxidation potential (1.922 V vs SHE) closely matching the experimental 1.964 V, and shows the A->D pathway is energetically downhill with a low barrier (TS1 at +3.38 kcal/mol). Substrates with strong electron-withdrawing groups, beta-methylstyrene, pyridine, and thiophene fail, consistent with the requirement to form a stabilized styryl radical cation on an electron-rich arene.
